EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds preceding an approaching squall line and cold front caused pockets of wind damage across most of Eastern Pennsylvania during the first half of the afternoon on the 18th.  Peak wind gusts averaged between 40 and 50 mph and knocked down tree limbs, weak trees and wires. The combination of the strong winds and squall line knocked out power to approximately 85,000 homes and businesses in Eastern Pennsylvania. In the Philadelphia Suburbs, PECO Energy outages were concentrated in Montgomery and Bucks Counties. Two school districts (Springfield and Spring-Ford) were closed the ensuing day (the 19th) because of the outages. All power was restored by the evening of the 19th. In the Poconos, Pennsylvania, Power and Light outages were concentrated in Monroe County.  Peak wind gusts included 46 mph in Mount Pocono (Monroe County), 45 mph at the Philadelphia International Airport and 41 mph at the Lehigh Valley International Airport.  The strong south winds in combination with high astronomical spring tides coming off the new moon produced minor tidal flooding during the afternoon high tide cycle along tidal sections of the Delaware River.  In Philadelphia,  the high tide reached 8.4 feet above mean lower low water. Minor tidal flooding begins at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.

On the afternoon of July 26th, a significant severe weather event impacted portions of the Binghamton, NY forecast area. By the late morning/early afternoon, a warm front had lifted north through the region placing much of the Binghamton forecast area in a moderately unstable airmass. Aloft, multiple approaching upper-level shortwave troughs approached the region from the central Great Lakes during the early afternoon hours which provided the initial forcing for thunderstorm development. Also, a punch of mid-level dry air helped create mid-level convective instability along with a favorable setup for strong damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down.

On the afternoon of July 26th, a significant severe weather event impacted portions of the Binghamton, NY forecast area. By the late morning/early afternoon, a warm front had lifted north through the region placing much of the Binghamton forecast area in a moderately unstable airmass. Aloft, multiple approaching upper-level shortwave troughs approached the region from the central Great Lakes during the early afternoon hours which provided the initial forcing for thunderstorm development. Also, a punch of mid-level dry air helped create mid-level convective instability along with a favorable setup for strong damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and wires were blown down in the Sayre and Athens area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another unseasonably hot and humid air mass affected Eastern Pennsylvania on the 17th and 18th. High temperatures on the 17th reached into the mid to upper 90s in most places with afternoon heat indices near 100F.  On July 18th, the combination of scorching high temperatures (near 100 degrees) and higher dew points produced hourly afternoon heat indices that reached between 106F and 111F, with the highest in Reading (Berks County).  The hot weather ended as a backdoor cold front slowly sagged south through the state on the night of the 18th. There was one heat related death in Philadelphia, a 77-year-old man. Highest temperatures included 100 degrees at the Philadelphia International Airport and Reading (Berks County), 99 degrees in Quakertown (Bucks County), 98 degrees in Allentown (Lehigh County), Doylestown (Bucks County) and Pottstown (Montgomery County), 97 degrees in West Chester (Chester County) and even 94 degrees in Mount Pocono (Monroe County).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow squall line intensified as it surged eastward across central and eastern Pennsylvania during the late morning and early afternoon. The thin line of low-topped storms, without cloud-to-ground lightning detected, stretched from New York to Kentucky along a strong cold front. The cold frontal squall line produced widespread wind damage across central Pennsylvania and adjacent Mid-Atlantic states. The Storm Prediction Center had highlighted the damaging wind threat a day in advance with a moderate risk categorical convective outlook.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph snapped a half-dozen large trees on a farm near Seven Valleys.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong instability and deep moisture combined with a vigorous upper-level disturbance over the Lower Great Lakes and weak frontal boundary/inverted lee trough east of the Blue Ridge Mountains to produce isolated severe thunderstorms and localized heavy rainfall during the late afternoon and early evening hours. Slow steering motions aloft allowed thunderstorms to remain nearly stationary and produce flash flooding in the  Williamsport area.
Scattered strong to severe thunderstorms developed across western Pennsylvania, northern West Virginia, and eastern Ohio ahead of a fast moving cold front during the morning and early afternoon.
Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused flash flooding of smaller streams in northeastern Northampton County.  Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 3 and 4 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused poor drainage flooding as well as flash flooding of smaller streams in Upper Mount Bethel Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Pre-frontal convection developed early in the day across western Pennsylvania and produced isolated wind damage and marginally severe hail. Additional strong to severe thunderstorms formed later in the afternoon and evening across portions of central and eastern Pennsylvania. This activity became more widespread and produced multiple reports of large hail and damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down trees in Westmont.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frontal system moved east from the Ohio Valley and triggered numerous strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. A few storms produced isolated reports of large hail and damaging winds. A cluster of training thunderstorms produced heavy rainfall across portions of the lower Susquehanna Valley. Localized flash flooding occurred over eastern Cumberland County where radar and rain gage reports indicated 1-2 inches fell in less than one hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential thunderstorm rains of 1-2 inches in less than an hour produced numerous reports of flash flooding across the eastern half of Cumberland County. Flash flooding closed SR 42 near Mt. Holly Springs, South Front and Market Streets in Lemoyne, SR 11 in Silver Springs Township and the 400 block of Front Street in Hampden Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was the focus for scattered severe thunderstorms across portions of western Pennsylvania. A slow moving thunderstorm produced isolated flash flooding over Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported trees down across roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was the focus for scattered severe thunderstorms across portions of western Pennsylvania. A slow moving thunderstorm produced isolated flash flooding over Garrett county Maryland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported several trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold frontal boundary pushed southeast from Lake Erie into a very moist and unstable air mass residing over central Pennsylvania. The frontal lift combined with high instability and adequate wind shear triggered numerous severe thunderstorms from the late morning through the afternoon. Multiple reports of thunderstorm wind damage and large hail were recorded.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down large trees Carlisle Springs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving upper level circulation moved from western Virgina east-northeast into the Chesapeake Bay/Delmarva region. This system tapped into a narrow axis of deep moisture and instability to trigger scattered thunderstorms across northern Maryland and south-central Pennsylvania. Storms moved north-northwest as they crossed the Mason-Dixon line. Wind shear profiles were marginally favorable for rotating low-topped storms. An EF0 tornado was confirmed near Greencastle in south-central Franklin County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down around 1356pm EDT along the 13000 block of Williamsport Pike in Antrim township; approximately 1.5 miles south-southwest of Greencastle. Damage at this location consisted of roofing material blown off of a barn and a toppled farm wagon. The EF0 tornado skipped northward on the west-side of Greencastle, knocking down trees and limbs along Williamson Road. A corn field was flattened just beyond the Greencastle city limits near Craig Road. The EF0 tornado continued to skip northward producing sporadic damage and finally lifted up around 1407pm EDT near the intersection of Williamson and Guitner Roads, where it removed a portion of a house roof. The estimated path length and width was approximately 6 miles long and 100 yards at its widest point. The estimated maximum wind speed was 85 mph, rating the tornado at the top-end of the EF0 wind speed range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved across the Upper Ohio Valley on July 3rd.  Scattered showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the front.  A few of the thunderstorms became severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed many trees.  Much of the town lost power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful squall line of strong to severe thunderstorms moved through Eastern Pennsylvania during the very late afternoon and first half of the evening on the 26th. Over 17,000 homes and businesses lost power because of downed trees, tree limbs and wires.  More than 10,000 still did not have power on the morning of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed a couple of trees In Whitehall Township. One large tree fell onto and damaged the brick structure of a home on Oakwood Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front racing across the Upper Ohio Valley produced scattered severe thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The 911 call center reported trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of thunderstorms with torrential downpours affected parts of southeastern Pennsylvania mainly during the first half of the day on the 3rd. Adjusted Doppler Radar storm total estimates exceeded 4 inches in western sections of Delaware County and eastern sections of Chester County.. The heavy rain was caused by the combination of a nearby stationary front and the approaching remnants associated with Hurricane Isaac.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding of smaller creeks as well as poor drainage and roadway flooding in Concord and Thornbury Townships. Doppler Radar rainfall estimates were around four inches within the townships..

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a weak cold front and upper air disturbance triggered scattered strong to severe thunderstorms, which produced damaging winds, small hail, and areas of heavy rain across portions of eastern Pennsylvania during the afternoon and evening of the 9th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees in South Coventry Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered scattered severe thunderstorms from the afternoon through the late evening. The storms produced marginally severe hail and localized heavy downpours that resulted in isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains from slow-moving thunderstorms produced flash flooding south of Morris. Rain gage reports indicated 2-4 inches fell over a short duration. The flooding closed a portion of SR 414 and caused a rock-slide along SR 287.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Successive waves of thunderstorms with very heavy rain associated with the remnants of Hurricane Isaac overnight on the 3rd into the evening of the 4th produced pockets of flash flooding in Eastern Pennsylvania.  By far the worst flash flooding occurred in Monroe County where Doppler Radar storm total estimates exceeded 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm with heavy rain caused flash flooding along the Pennypack Creek as well as poor drainage flooding in northeastern Philadelphia. The Pennypack Creek at the Rhawn Street Bridge was above its 7 foot flood stage from 203 p.m. EDT through 239 p.m. EDT on the 4th. It crested at 7.78 feet at 215 p.m. EDT. Of the three times the Pennypack Creek flooded on the 4th, this was its highest crest of the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms produced flash flooding mainly across Pittsburgh and Allegheny county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported flash flooding in Dravosburg on SR 837.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary was the focus for scattered severe thunderstorms across portions of western Pennsylvania. A slow moving thunderstorm produced isolated flash flooding over Garrett county Maryland.
A slow moving area of low pressure produced scattered severe thunderstorms.

Showers and thunderstorms developed along a cold front as it moved across Pennsylvania.  As the storms moved into northeast Pennsylvania, they produced areas of wind damage and large hail. Two minor injuries resulted when winds in excess of 60 mph damaged a bandstand at the fairgrounds in Luzerne county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branches were downed, blocking both lanes of Route 92.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact, intense line of thunderstorms (derecho) produced multiple reports of straight-line wind damage across central Pennsylvania as it tracked southeast from the northwest mountains into the lower Susquehanna Valley. The line of thunderstorms initiated near Lake Erie shortly after midnight and become stronger and more organized as they moved southeast through the predawn hours before exiting the area around 5am EDT.||A second thunderstorm complex developed in the Ohio Valley later that afternoon and produced large hail and damaging winds across portions of southern Pennsylvania during the evening.

A squall line that preceded a cold front produced strong to locally severe thunderstorms in Eastern Pennsylvania during the evening of the 29th. The worst reported wind damage was in the Lehigh Valley where approximately 3,200 homes and businesses lost power. Power was not fully restored until the 30th in Lehigh County and the 31st in Northampton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few large tree limbs and wires South Whitehall Township. About 1,287 Pennsylvania Power and Light customers lost power in the township. The number dropped to 300 by Midnight EDT that night.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of July 23rd, an upper level disturbance moved east toward northeast Pennsylvania from southern Ontario, Canada.  Daytime heating and instability combined with favorable wind parameters in the atmosphere to produce severe thunderstorms.  Many reports were received of wind damage and very large hail.

During the afternoon of July 23rd, an upper level disturbance moved east toward northeast Pennsylvania from southern Ontario, Canada.  Daytime heating and instability combined with favorable wind parameters in the atmosphere to produce severe thunderstorms.  Many reports were received of wind damage and very large hail.

A strong frontal system with abundant moisture brought a period of heavy rainfall and damaging thunderstorm winds to portions of the lower Susquehanna Valley. The intense thunderstorm rains produced localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential thunderstorm rains caused flash flooding along small streams and creeks in the southern part of the county. The flooding also closed many roads and resulted in several water rescues.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of Severe Thunderstorms moved across the northern tier of Central Pennsylvania during the evening hours of May 2nd, producing large hail and pockets of wind damage. A weak EF0 tornado was also confirmed in Potter County near Ayers Hill.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked down several trees and power lines on Route 446 in Keating Township. The winds also flipped a large camper on its side.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed along a slow moving cold front from eastern Ohio, to northern West Virginia and western Pennsylvania. Heavy rainfall from a few storms also produced isolated flash flooding.

A protracted period of rain that included embedded thunderstorms with heavy downpours caused flash flooding in sections of northern Lehigh County as well as in the Poconos. Rain began during the early morning on the 18th, but fell heavily in thunderstorms during the mid to late afternoon on the 18th when flash flooding occurred. The rain became lighter and more intermittent during the evening of the 18th and ended overnight. Event precipitation totals averaged 3 to 6 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused flash flooding of smaller streams and poor drainage flooding in northern Lehigh County. Event precipitation totals included 2.76 inches in Schnecksville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A compact, intense line of thunderstorms (derecho) produced multiple reports of straight-line wind damage across central Pennsylvania as it tracked southeast from the northwest mountains into the lower Susquehanna Valley. The line of thunderstorms initiated near Lake Erie shortly after midnight and become stronger and more organized as they moved southeast through the predawn hours before exiting the area around 5am EDT.||A second thunderstorm complex developed in the Ohio Valley later that afternoon and produced large hail and damaging winds across portions of southern Pennsylvania during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds estimated near 60 mph knocked down a large 30-inch diameter tree near Narvon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A squall line that preceded a cold front produced strong to locally severe thunderstorms in Eastern Pennsylvania during the evening of the 29th. The worst reported wind damage was in the Lehigh Valley where approximately 3,200 homes and businesses lost power. Power was not fully restored until the 30th in Lehigh County and the 31st in Northampton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few large tree limbs and  wires in Nazareth.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread thunderstorms developed across central Pennsylvania during the afternoon.  Several of these storms became severe producing damaging winds and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail reported near Linden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe thunderstorms developed across western Pennsylvania as a strong cold front moved across the Upper Ohio Valley.
Severe thunderstorms produced isolated wind damage, large hail and flash flooding across portions of the mid and lower Susquehanna Valley during the afternoon.
A cold front moved into a very warm and unstable air mass over central Pennsylvania, which triggered numerous severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail reported near Amity Hall.
